While it's a bit heavy-handed (and convenient) to turn the Azor Ahai stuff into a secret that's passed down from from king to heir ... it also makes quite a lot of sense. Targaryans seem to be heavily into their dreams and into prophecy, so it's not that far fetched to assume they would take Aegon's dream seriously like that. Plus if it really came from GRRM himself, who are we to question it..? :)
What I'm not happy with is that it kinda, sorta canonizes what happened in season 8 of GoT, as you could now understand the prophecy to refer to not a man or a woman but to the freakin' dagger that Arya uses to kill the Night King. Making it irrelevant who sits the throne or even who wields that blade, as long as someone uses it to attack and kill the right ice zombie with it. 
And while I'm not even caught up with the books that are out there, I refuse to believe that it was GRRM's idea that the tool to end the long night is more important than the people who can/will do it. 
I'm not sure we'll ever see an ending to this story, as George hasn't managed to even finish WoW yet (after ... 13? years) and he isn't getting any younger - but if we do get A Dream of Spring, I'm almost certain its ending will be much less of a disappointment than what D&D cranked out while they were already eyeing that Disney-gig ...

I really liked the episode, but there was one element that jar'd me out the narrative for 10minutes or more, it was so dumb to me. But no one is noticing it. However you mentioning Daemons behaviour comes closest so let me write it out here.

Doesn't anyone have a problem with how Daemon cheated in the joust? I mean he pretty much stopped just short of skewering his opponents horse with the lance? and he did it in such obvious purposeful fashion? Lets set aside for the moment how EXPENSIVE trained warhorses were in medieval times, how are there no consequences for that reckless and obviously unchivalrous cheating? 

If there are no consequences for such reckless action that could EASILY result in the DEATH of noble families SONS (which they LOVE and strive to have above all), why would any of them joust? why would any of them bother showing up for a contest of "skill" if reckless cheating is allowed? why would they even bother to accept ANY of a cheaters challenges. And when they refuse to joust (because the cheater might be the prince-heir against whom they DARE not counter cheat to protect themselves), it would be a massive stain on the cheaters reputation, because their actions and station would force other nobles sons to decline to joust.

I really don't see that behaviour of Daemon as possible. Joust etiquette would have long ago weeded out that sort of behaviour with significant consequences, and the nobility would react accordingly. But here, they all seem deranged, clapping for the cheating, and even Rhaenyra saying "well played uncle"?? its delusional. I don't see it EVER being possible that a nobility-social hierarchy would tolerate that behaviour. They should have all been shocked and appalled. And Hightower should have been showing great displeasure toward the king if the King did not publically condemn the action, etc, etc.

@ericaugust1501  yeah, for example, a century later in the Dunk and Egg series, Aerion targaryen, second son of prince Maekar, killed the horse of someone who he was jousting against in a tourney, and not only was disqualified, but had to give his horse in compensation.

The way you mention the tournament as being host to politics reminds me of an article I read last week (ergo, is fresh in my mind). Robb's wife in the show mentions that she didn't want to be a "proper lady" having fancy features and tournaments and wearing pretty dresses. And this shows her ignorance of politics, because feasts and tournaments are not just for the sake of festivity, they're shows of power. The rich and powerful throw feasts to show they can afford to throw away good food on guests, and tournaments give warriors a chance to show off their skill and strength in front of an audience of powerful nobles who might be impressed by them. If a nobody warrior with no last name wins a tourney where all the big name houses are competing, it would reflect very badly on them and the winner may be seen as insulting them, which may make them friends or enemies. And of course clothes are used to show off a family's wealth and also allegiances, if the Lannisters all wear red and gold then all their loyal men will be wearing red and gold, and if a member of their family happened to show up wearing the colors of the Tyrells or the Martells, it would be a scandal and people would gossip about why and what it could mean.

So for Robb's wife to talk about her disinterest in tournaments and feasts and clothes not only shows obvious misogyny from the writers, who feel they have to show that Robb's wife is "worthy" because she's not a "normal" girl and rejects womanly things, but also her ignorance - and theirs - about the subtle intricacies of politics and nobility and power. To anyone who knew better, it would be a big warning sign that this woman is unfit to be a Queen.

I'm all for reasonable artistic license, but the TV show's decision to include Weirwood trees in the Red Keep was a disservice to Martin. An invading army from the east (Targaryens) subduing the native population and destroying their religious iconography is analogous to the European conquest of the New World, and Martin's readers would have recognized that. Be careful when adapting other people's work and treat details with respect.

Yes, only a poor mother would not be willing to end her life, possibly through a torturous and gruesome death, for a yet to be born baby. 

I would agree that media has plenty of examples of pregnant women choosing their unborn children over their own health, but that has more to do with pregnant woman, and mothers in general, in media being cast as saintly martyrs instead of real human beings. 

Would you be willing to be cut open, while alive, for a sixth pregnancy of a child you were obligated to produce? Would you like your life valued less than an unborn baby? Would you want to die, leaving your 14 year old daughter, a child you know and love, without her mother?

Also, historically, unborn children, however wanted, were not prioritized over a mother's life. Pragmatically it did not make sense due to high infant mortality rates. 

You may be surprised to know that a mother's health is also still prioritized over a pregnancy today as well.

Two corrections.  First the choice that Viserys had during the birth was to attempt to save the baby or lose both.  Aemma was going to die regardless, but there was a slight possibility that they could save her son.  

Second is in regards to the joust.  I am taking reference from real historical events, since we don't know much about the jousts and tournaments in GoT.  The "rules" were not necessarily standardized early on.  In fact, the early tourneys were just mock battles, where contestants would be divided between two groups (or armies) and then they would start a melee.  However, some tournaments had more rules than others.  The rules (or lack thereof) were determined by the one sanctioning or organizing the tournament and the rules varied from tournament to tournament.  So until we know what the rules are in regards to GoT tourneys it is safe to say that Daemon tripping the horse and the killings you mentioned were in fact allowed by the rules.

It's pretty cool symbolically that the Velaryons use a seahorse as their sigil. Seahorses are in the same family as seadragons, and in some eastern cultures and myths, seahorses are seen as relatives of dragons - baby dragons sometimes resemble seahorses, and the Japanese word for seahorse is "tatsu-no-otoshigo", which literally translates as "dragon's bastard child." By using a seahorse as their sigil, the Velaryons are saying they too, are dragons, but they are dragons of the sea, not of the sky. It also fits their ambitions to constantly marry into House Targaryen, a constant reminder that they aren't REAL dragons, but have the potential to become dragons through marriage and conquest.

One pities the state education has fallen into. In every day speech the word queen is used. However this is usually a queen consort. Queen consorts derive the title by marriage. Example Queen Camilla. When Charles dies she will technically be dowager Queen but in everyday use will still be called Queen Camilla. When a woman inherits the throne in her own right she is a Queen regnant. Examples are Queen Eliabeth or Queen Victoria. In recent tradition the spouse of a ruling Queen is the prince consort. This goes back to the time of Queen Anne

Some of this episode makes no sense.

The war is going badly for Daemon & co.... except that they have the enemy blockaded on a small island, with dragons forcing them to hide in their caves. Sounds like the war is going excellently! Just starve them out FFS. How they were able to kidnap people from that position is unknowable, unless maybe the crabfeeder has some of Greyjoy's magic teleporting ships from season 8?

The king says he would look weak by helping Daemon in his war... except that if he used the full royal might he would win the war that his brother was losing - showing empathy, strength, and gratitude (from the Valeryons at least). Winning a war to save family is literally the ideal way to show (military) might without appearing like a blood thirsty warmonger. It's a golden opportunity for any Kings, portraying it as a difficult dilemma is utterly immersion breaking. 

The "revelation" that Rhaenyra should marry Laenor is the most obvious marriage possible. Viserys should have countered with that last episode, as soon as he turned down marriage with Laena. Unless, all the way back in episode 1, Daemon had divorced his absentee wife and married Rhaenyra himself. These are all blindingly obvious solutions to the dynastic problem at hand, it's only the need to keep the plot unresolved that somehow makes these things not forgone conclusions.

That's not to mention the stupidity of holding a war council in front of the entire army. What a great way to prevent frank advice and discussion, all the while discouraging the troops!

It invokes similar beach battles like Normandy Bay, where many died, but many survived and still rushed forward to conquer the beach. It's also  harder to hit a single moving target than to fire indiscriminately into a group of warriors. Plenty of lone warriors have accomplished feats against many, some by utilizing choke points and others by never staying still, constantly on the offensive. Even still, it makes sense that Daemon's reckless charge requires an air strike to save him, similarly to how Tolkien used the eagles as a fantasy air force, delivering salvation for warriors who thought all might be lost, like how WWI soldiers would have felt when air strikes seemingly brought salvation from the heavens and doom upon their enemies.

Was anyone else concerned with Rhaenyra's night with Criston? Don't get me wrong, I like the relationship between the two of them, but something about that scene felt off, and I think that was intentional. Just before Rhaenyra seduces Criston, she almost enthusiastically screws Daemon, and when he backs out, she becomes irritated and spends the night with Criston instead. While she makes Criston feel like he's the one she wants, he was really her second option — is that fair to him? I think that scene showed that while Rhaenyra's feelings for Criston are genuine, her decision to seduce and sleep with him was born of an overly-emotional reaction to her frustrations and needs – like when she kept on stabbing the boar in episode 3, even though it was clearly dead after her first few stabs. Her need to get what she wants (from Daemon or Criston) is also rather self-centered, much like how she dismisses the opinions of the commoners. It makes me wonder if Rhaenyra's selfish tendency to give in to her emotions will result in her downfall.
Also, at 4:06, the fish appears to be the sigil of House Mooton, not of House Tully.

I actually sympathise with Criston. 

He wasn't a noble, he had to earn his spot. He had finally been given a title only for it to effectively be destroyed (as a kings guard he can never create a noble lineage now). He tried his best to act within the codes of chivalry in a world where nobody actually follows it. He has his vows broken because (if we leave aside all the nobility and ceremony for a second) his boss took advantage of him by leveraging her power to have sex with him. He now has to live in constant fear of being murdered (since he hasn't a big noble family behind him that's an actual risk). Despite this he does fall in love with her only for her to tell him he can never be anything but a side piece due to her station. He tries to find a solution where they can be together and she just says that her job is more important than him. He's now stuck basically being a dick appointment except with the risk of being killed if it ever came to light. Then he gets threatened by someone who says if he doesn't continue to maintain this secret then he will be outed (and thus killed).

Even though I understand Alicent's frustration, I think she was poisoned by her father's ambition.  He pushed his daughter into a manipulation to make his family part of the royalty & as he got exposed & kicked out the door (could've been much worse) he plants a poison seed in her head that's only grown more, because of her anger that Rhaenyra lied to her, that Rhaenyra will kill her children to keep the throne.  Alicent has painted Rhaenyra, in her mind, as a deceiver & untrustworthy so why WOULDN'T she do that to keep the throne?  But, I don't see Rhaenyra wanting anything like that.  She lied because the consequences to her could've been fatal, not because of blind ambition for the throne. She never particularly wanted it, but I think Viserys telling her about the Song of Ice and Fire has made her feel more responsible and there is a bit of pride now that her father has stuck with her as heir & not sought to replace her.  But, I haven't seen her shown vicious intentions towards anyone. She seems to just want to live her life as she wishes with as little complications as possible & killing someone else's children (let alone someone's who used to be her best/only friend's children) is probably the furthest thing from her mind. But, there's no way to prove that since she is a liar and not to be trusted, in Alicent's eyes. I thought her offer to Alicent was probably the best thing she could've done, given her situation, but it wasn't good enough since her children are illegitimate. So, she bounced. I get that & Alicent's poisoned mind is poisoning her children, harboring a murdering loose cannon in Criston and has gotten the Hand & his 1st born son killed.  So, I'm going to have to side with Rhaenyra, at this point.

Viserys could end the conflict with a few simple commands, but as we have seen, he is not a good king; he is weak and allows everyone to manipulate his actions.  Alicent has clearly been stoking the flames of this conflict starting with that green dress, and now with her accusations (though accurate) about Rhaenera's bastards..erm children.  Viserys could easily end that conflict by telling Alicent that any further attempts by her to manipulate the kingdom into revolt against his heir will result in a Charge of Sedition and her execution for it.  But, as I said, Viserys is too weak a ruler to ever make that command happen.  He loves Alicent, and has hopes that this conflict will just go away.  So, this is all Viserys' fault and all the bloodshed to follow.

I feel like you're a bit disengenuous here.  This situation is nothing like the issue with Cersei's kids.  In ASOIAF Cersei is attempting to pass off children with no royal blood or claim on the throne as royal, their bastard status feeds in to that, but the main problem is not their bastardry but their lack of any claim to the throne.  Here the largest issue is Viserys' willful blindness, his daughter's children have royal blood, they are the children of the heir to the throne, not the children of the husband of the heir to the throne.  Certainly the situation is one asking for problems and conflicts, but no one here is attempting to put someone with zero royal blood on the throne.  Jace and Luke are children of the heir, not children of the heir's husband/wife being passed off as children of the heir.

I'm not arguing that their aren't multitiude problems with the situation and that the choices these characters make aren't absolute shit, rather that you can't really conflate the situation from ASOIAF with the situation here.

"The few times they tried"
Translation: R made sure he got a few chances and nothing more. Had L been straight he would have fought more for his line to continue. Of course R is at this point an accomplished liar. About the only true statement in that interaction is that L is a good man. And she's right, he's good and naive and, what's worse, gaslit by an unscrupulous spouse.

Let's not even address how tone-deaf the statement about fairness is in this scenario. When the male fucks around the wife still gets her biological children on the succession tree. When female fucks around, she ends up effectively stealing her husband's birthright and ensuring the end of his line. To make this as clear as possible. Had the genders been swapped, L would have gotten a much better deal than he is getting now. 
And at this point nobody believes any of R's children are legitimate so every fear the lords of Westeros had about a woman king seem to be validated. Had she had trueborn sons it would have allowed for the lords to start treating both children as perspective leaders which would have ensured that the path to a female leader would not be viewed as negatively in the future.

The fact that the books make it clear that L dies is a much better option mostly because of how poorly the show presents the scene. The only way that scene would have worked would have been if the fire was strong enough and there would have been some reasonable explanation as to why the body got into the hearth entirely.

The writers try to be clever but due to the way they ultimately handle it, they fail to follow through. Think of the death of Daemon's first wife. We get an off statement that she had her head crushed (making it clear he used the rock as a cudgel) which would make no sense for a fall. Instead think of what would have happened if we would have gotten a scene where D takes the rock and places it under her head and we see the defiance falter as a look of terror goes through his wife's face as she realizes she's going to get her skull cracked and her killer will escape completely. We hear a crack and the screen fades to black. This would go much further to emphasize how much of a villain D can be under the right circumstances.

Lastly, I enjoyed the portrayal of Viserys's deterioration and final days and hours. I am a doctor, it is not uncommon for terminally ill patients to rally in their final days. I have seen patients who are on death's door, suddenly stand up and walk only to die a few days later. 

His symptoms in his final moments emphasise the importance of palliative care. People think palliative care means you just give up and let the patient die, but actually It's about symptom control. Basically we all know that Viserys is going to die, he isn't suddenly going to be cured, the disease is terminal. 

Modern doctors use opiates for pain relief and breathlessness, benzodiazepines for anxiety, anti-emetics for nausea and glycopyrronium for secretions (as you die, the body starts to produce secretions which can compromise the airway - this is what causes the "death rattle" sound which can be disturbing for the patient and family members). We also see that he is delirious, sometimes lucid and sometimes confused. Fluctuating confusion is a hallmark of delirium, which can be triggered by infection, dehydration, constipation and a variety of other causes, in this case due to his terminal illness. 

When Rhaenyra and Daemon were arguing with Alicent about the milk of the poppy and the treatments given by the maester, that reminded me of a common scenario I would see on the wards. When a patient is in their final days, otherwise distant family members may appear and there often is a lot of conflict over what is right for the patient and calling into question other people's decision making. However it misses the forest for the trees in this case, it doesn't matter how much milk of the poppy you're giving to Viserys - he's dying. It's over. Different family members come to accept this at different points in time, some faster than others.

I did feel really bad for him when he was lying there in agony and Rhaenyra and Daemon barged in to complain about Alicent. It was also very tragic when that happened again but this time it was Rhaenyra begging her dying father to help her. What a tremendous act of love and strength by Viserys, that even when he was so close to death he stood and wore his crown and sat on the throne one last time. Very touching scene.

Also have to echo the praise for Paddy. I know that the show is controversial and Ryan Condall is often criticised, not entirely unfairly (however I do think the way Sarah Hess is spoken about by the fandom is absolutely reprehensible, aside from the aspersions cast on her personal life, she's a writer for select episodes, she's not the show runner. That's Condall. She doesn't decide broad plot points and directions like deaging Alicent and the romantic implications with Rhaenyra - that was not her decision). 

But I do think that there were parts of the show that are much more interesting than fire and blood. Viserys is a far more complex and compelling character compared to the jolly two-dimensional flat character in the book. Aemond is more compelling too with a pathos that the book couldn't convey. Rhaenyra's relationship with Laenor in the show is sweet. Criston Cole I find to be far more compelling in the show, I really enjoy how Fabien portrays his barely contained rage. I prefer Halaena as a dreamer. Aegon is very compelling as portrayed by Tom Glynn Carney. And lastly, I do think de-aging Alicent was a compelling decision, it's so much more intriguing and challenging to deal with your best friend marrying your dad not long after your mom died. I think that's far more interesting than the evil stepmother that Alicent is in the books.

Most of these areas where the show is superior is due to the superlative performances of the actors who really cannot be praised enough. But I don't think Condall's decisions were entirely bad either. I've been enjoying the show even when it deviates from the book, and of course it's important to note that the book was always going to be difficult to adapt due to its unreliable narration format.

I thought that scene with Alicent and Dyanna was very interesting. At first I thought it was showing Alicent's virtue but as the scene continued, it got darker. Alicent manipulated Dyanna into silence. Not that Dyanna was planning on going around telling everyone, but Alicent basically couched a threat in nicer words. It reminded me of how Rhaenyra said she was hiding her ambition behind righteousness. 

I don't know if what Alicent was saying would necessarily resonate with Dyanna, amongst the peasantry there was less of a concept of virtue per se - in Tess of the D'urbervilles, Hardy notes that a peasant man might have laughed off/dismissed Tess' lack of virginity compared to a religious, upper class man like Angel Clare. The faith held more of a grip on the upper classes, rather like how the middle classes are the most religious class today. 

One thing I did like about the scene is that r*pe was acknowledged as a moral wrong. People think that in the mediaeval ages anything goes but that isn't true. R*pe was considered a crime and people could be punished for it. Of course nobles could easily get away with their crimes, particularly as they had more power and privilege for cover ups such as Alicent's cover up here - but If it had been discovered, most people would think that he had done something wrong even in the mediaeval era.

Frankly, Allicent is still the furthest thing from sympathetic to me. She just looks lika a more pious-wannabe less sociopathic version of Cersei. Which is still firmly in the "absolute bitch" zone as far as I'm concerned. Whereas Rhaenyra actually matured with the passing years, Allicent became more and more selfish and entitled, to the point where she thinks she can scream at and defy her own husband (i.e. THE KING OF WESTEROS), steal his Valyrian dagger and try to maim his own grandsons and daughter.

One of the things I love about this show is that they really pinpoint that yes, Rhaenyra and Daemon are flawed, but the Greens are the bad guys because they plunge an entire realm into war based on petty, personal prejudices and grudges and barefaced shameless hypocrisy, while masquerading as being the righteous ones and not a single true concern about the future of the realm. Aegon's coronation is essentially the Hightowers becoming the shadow rulers behind the throne.

And at the same time, the whole point of the show is portraying what the REAL History of the Dance of Dragons was like, rather than RECORDED History such as Mushroom's record (which, naturally, is extremely biased or ignorant towards the finer and secret details of what was truly going on with these people).

As for what beasts will emerge from beneath the boards? Maybe they'd like the Song of Blood and Cheese...

The first reason was because it took him a long time to get a dragon. All the other kids in King’s Landing claimed dragons, or had them hatch, when they were very young. Aegon hatched Sunfyre, Helaena bonded Dreamfyre, Daeron hatched Tessarion (although they were in Oldtown), Jace hatched Vermax, Luke hatched Arrax, and Joffrey hatched Tyraxes. And even one of the girls, Baela, hatched Moondancer, although she was in Pentos. 

So that was a big part of it. The second main reason was probably because prior to losing his eye and bonding Vhagar, Aemond was something of a teacher’s pet (which as any kid who’s ever gone to school before knows makes you the target of bullying). He learned Valyrian fluently, he educated himself in the histories, and probably most importantly, he was not obsessed with girls or sex the way Aegon was. Its implied that Aemond is on the asexual spectrum, that he’s only comfortable being intimate with women he knows well. Aegon hated the idea that he had to marry Helaena, not bc she’s his sister, but bc she’s “weird” and “an idiot”. Whereas Aemond was much more comfortable with the idea, and wished that Alicent had betrothed Helaena to him, rather than Aegon. Later on we learn that Aemond slept with Silvi (the head madam at the brothel) to lose his virginity (and its implied Aegon pressured him into this), and when Aemond returns to the brothel, he only wants to be with Silvi instead of any other girls who might be younger or prettier. This seeming lack of sexual confidence is something boys are often bullied for. And we see in a later episode of Season 2 that Aegon is still bullying Aemond for his seeming lack of sexuality. He does so in a very public and brutal way, and he’s very casual about it, so I imagine it’s something he does a lot.

I find Criston Cole's role in the story so interesting in so many ways, as while he is certainly a villanous character, I don't think he's neccesarily malicious. He's quite simple in so many regards: he's meant to embody the classical low-born heroe archetype turned bad, after all. While he's petty and self-destructive, its quite funny to see how many of his actions end up making it worse for him. He joined the kingsguard so now he can't marry, and any singular one of his crimes is enough to get him executed; he broke his oath for Rahenyra only to be sidestepped by politics; his impulsive decision to send Arryk on a suicide mission has now placed him in an even more dangerous and demanding position; and because he never accomplishes anything in any way, his impotence only grows stronger. He's dangerous but not because of his strength or his mind, but because of his ability to make awful decisions. Or maybe, it's just piss-poor bad luck.

It’s implied in GOT Season 2 that you’d have to be extremely rich in order to repair Harrenhal enough to make it truly livable. Like it would take almost the entire Lannister fortune rich. But, bc all the Houses that are wealthy enough to afford to fix Harrenhal already have other castles of their own (and the realm has been at peace for decades) so why bother spending such a fortune? Plus, many people believe that Harrenhal is truly cursed, and I’d be leery about spending an entire kingdom’s worth of gold to try and fix a haunted house, you know? 

And this is my own speculation, but I wouldn’t be surprised if part of the reason is also because the peasants would revolt if a lord tried to force them to fix/rebuild the castle. When Harren the Black first had the castle built, hundreds, maybe even thousands, of peasants died in the construction bc the work was so dangerous, and he could force them into such work bc at the time, peasants had zero rights. After Aegon’s Conquest, and especially during King Jahaerys reign, peasants and smallfolk were given more rights, instead of being treated like slaves. It should be noted that in both instances, it was the Queens who were mostly responsible for giving the smallfolk the few rights they do have.

The Burning Mill cut scene was immaculate & got the point across about the brutality of war...Feast for Crows. It was also baiting & switching the audience for the actual battle next episode. GoT did it constantly in s2 before Blackwater. Overloading with battles takes away the novelty. Let the casuals who complained about it stick to their mindless action flicks. Meanwhile targ stans mad about Ulf & Baelon? Oh no, a royal prince sired a bastard & water is wet! Where did they think those dragonseeds came from? Look at the timeline even in the book & it's clear some rather recent Targaryen in KL was fooling around. Helaena's "I forgive you" is a prophetic present tense literary device. She's talking about something that hasn't happened yet...the window. Brilliant writing! Finally a depiction of Harrenhal that actually gives it the look & feel of a cursed, haunted castle. The weirdwood reaching out to the Isle of Faces, yearning for its kind, almost! Goomer Daemon's ideal vision of Rhaenyra being stuck at 15 year old. Scary. The "I swear to you on the memory of my mother" clapback is the best line of the series so far. And lastly, Rhaenyra is not whitewashed. Her treatment of Rhaena was a sign that Rhaenyra will ultimately be submerged by the system she's trying to change. She will leave everyone disillusioned, which is the point they're making about the immutability of autocracy. Rhaenyra's subtle selfishness & ego has been so beautifully characterized. Placing her sons & heirs above all, asking her own orphan step child to play surrogate, all in the place where said child lost her own mother. No remorse & a complete cognitive dissonance. Rhaenyra is such a triumph of adaptation I have so much faith they’re going to manage to successfully pull off her tragedy. She hypocritically outsources motherhood to Rhaena & assigns her that passive feminine role. As a character Rhaenyra is most fascinating when she's forced to navigate & perpetuate the gendered structures she despises & wishes she could transcend. Rhaena is clearly straining against the fact that if she has no dragon she is to be relegated to childcare while Baela gets at least some freedom (or the illusion of it). The show is giving Rhaena a nuanced dimension to be dissatisfied with this position (the book doesn't give her much of anything). Conflict is good! & Rhaenyra’s central conflict— that to gain & retain power within a violent system one must perpetuate those violences, no matter one’s honorable intentions— having its seeds sown here is just really smart writing.

Interesting idea, but I imagine that a dragon would be able to smell if a prey animal was diseased or sick in some way. Plus dragons only eat cooked meat, and I imagine dragon fire could obliterate poison, like how cooking meat to temperature kills bacteria. Poisons also tend to be delicate mixtures, and heat can change how various compounds work, making them ineffective. 
Plus the dragons are guarded by dragon keepers- they surely would notice if a sheep or other prey animal was sick and cull it from the herd before the dragons had a chance to eat it.

She definitely was hurt of course, bc Rhaenys does love Corlys, but she’s also lived in a world where men are allowed to indulge in whatever pleasures they choose, but women must be chaste and restrained. Something that's flown under everyone's radar since last season is how the show subtly juxtaposes Rhaenys and Alicent's roles as mothers. Rhaenys may not have been the best spouse to Laenor like Alicent was to Viserys, but she's proven to be a more caring mother than her former friend. Alicent objectifies all her kids the way Otto objectified her, treating them not as people but as vessels for which she, Otto, and others can amass power. 
When Luke dies, Rhaenrya is absolutely devastated and heartbroken, whereas when Alicent's grandson gets killed, she's perturbed and feels guilty, but doesn't hesitate to go along with her dad's plan to capitalize on the death and use his decapitated body as propaganda against Rhanerya, and forces Helena (who's potentially autistic or on the spectrum) to participate in the traumatic procession to boost their support. 
In this episode, Rhanerya relies on her eldest son to run the war while she wallies in grief and trusts him enough to reveal the Song of Ice and Fire and why it's paramount that they make peace. Meanwhile, Alicent keeps Aegon in the dark about all the military plans and treats him like a passive, static item whose only purpose is to sit on a spiky chair while everyone else runs the realm.

Alternative take on Larys saying Lyonel blamed Alys for his disability: he’s lying. He has plenty of reasons to lie, from making himself look more sympathetic to Aegon, to further tarnishing the memory of his father, (which seemed to be a goal, as it is clear Larys hated his family), to to possibly wanting to sow seeds of suspicion against Alys. Just because Alys could have potentially been Larys’s mentor doesn’t mean he had any love for her. Bloodraven kind of proves the point that shitty, selfish people can become powerful magicians who just happen to do things that help the realm. It would make sense for Larys to see Alys as a rival and would take any change to usurp her, especially if she comes across as a more powerful magician. Westeros is a loose adaptation of the UK, and in history, many royals and noble houses used lions as their heraldry- in times of history where they wouldn’t have been able to travel to places like Africa. Orys Baratheon was thought to be Aegon’s bastard brother, not son, but it’s not been definitively proven, so there’s no way to know for sure. 
However the Baratheons do have some Targaryen genes, the two families have intermarried several times. In the books, Rhaenys Targaryen (the rider of Meleys) has black hair bc of her Baratheon  mother, Jocelyn. In fact, Robert Baratheon’s grandmother Rhaelle, was a Targaryen. 

So it is very possible that Baratheon’s could ride dragons. But the Targaryens obviously tried their best to keep the dragons within their own family, trying not to allow eggs to be given to only half-Targaryens, and if they did give eggs to half-Targaryens, it was specifically to draw those branches of the family closer to the throne, like with Rhaenys and Corlys’ children, Laenor and Laena.

@11:00 The quote refers to Dragonseeds as "happy bastards" because the smallfolk of Dragonstone and Driftmark viewed the Targaryens and Velaryons as being divine people or demigods, so to bear the child of one of them was to be blessed..... and not just blessed in a magical way, but also blessed with gifts of money and land to the mother and her family by the father of her bastard child. This further increased smallfolk maidens' willingness to lay with nobles of Valyrian families, regardless of if they were attracted to the nobles, simply as a matter of practicality. When you grow up a peasant, the idea of "if you bear a Targaryen or Velaryon bastard, you are not only the mother to a semi-divine child, but you and your family are rewarded generously and are able to live a bit more comfortably (or more than a bit, depending on the person giving the gifts,) and it ISN'T considered a stain on your honor that affects your ability to find a husband (like how in most of Westeros, bearing a bastard child is a HUGE faux-pas,)" it seems like a pretty good deal, and this meant Dragonseeds were plentiful on both islands

I only binged both seasons a couple of weeks ago for the first time and I haven't read Fire & Blood, but: Is it just me or do the showrunners of Hot-D seem to have less of a problem with prophecy and magic than D&D did? And while I'm still not 100% sure I needed that super on-the-nose connection between the two shows/eras that they wrote in, I really liked both seasons quite a lot. Even season 2, which lots of folks seem to be bashing for some reason, and its whole "Daemon's spooky adventures at Harrenhal" arc and also its conclusion. Plus that little scene with Alfred and the disembodied whisper was such a nice touch - seeing how they were standing in front of the Weirwood.
Overall, the show is not without its flaws and seems to have a somewhat narrower scope than GoT, but to me at least, it feels quite a lot more authentic and true to the source material than what we had to put up with in season 7 and 8 of GoT.
